item.title: Collaborative Optimization of Receptive Field and Texture Preservation for Remote Sensing Small Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Sainan Zhou
Qi Qi
Jianhui Wu
Wei He
Siyuan Chen
Bing Tu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Small object detection in remote sensing imagery remains a challenging task due to severe scale variations, weak texture characteristics, and complex background interference. Existing methods often fail to effectively balance receptive field expansion and texture preservation, resulting in degraded feature representations for small objects. To address this issue, we propose a collaborative optimization framework, termed the Receptive Field-Texture Preservation Network (RTP-Net), which enhances small object detection through three complementary modules: the Global-Local Feature Extraction Module (GLEM), the Adaptive Weighted Enhancement Module (AWEM), and the Multi-Scale Attention Fusion Module (MSAF). In particular, GLEM jointly models global contextual information and fine-grained local textures to alleviate feature degradation caused by scale variation. AWEM further enhances texture preservation and enhances discriminative capability via adaptive feature reweighting. In addition, MSAF improves multi-scale feature fusion by incorporating attention mechanisms, which enhance feature consistency while suppressing background interference and reducing misclassification in complex scenes.Extensive experiments conducted on multiple public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms several state-of-the-art detectors, particularly in small object detection tasks. Notably, RTP-Net achieves 84.1% mAP on the DIOR dataset, 95.3% mAP on the NWPU VHR-10 dataset, and 40.3% mAP on the AI-TOD dataset. These results validate the robustness and generalization capability of the proposed framework across diverse datasets and varying object scales. Published: 2026-07-22T19:04:09+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sainan Zhou; Qi Qi; Jianhui Wu; Wei He; Siyuan Chen; Bing Tu

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3715676

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Small object detection in remote sensing imagery remains a challenging task due to severe scale variations, weak texture characteristics, and complex background interference. Existing methods often fail to effectively balance receptive field expansion and texture preservation, resulting in degraded feature representations for small objects. To address this issue, we propose a collaborative optimization framework, termed the Receptive Field-Texture Preservation Network (RTP-Net), which enhances small object detection through three complementary modules: the Global-Local Feature Extraction Module (GLEM), the Adaptive Weighted Enhancement Module (AWEM), and the Multi-Scale Attention Fusion Module (MSAF). In particular, GLEM jointly models global contextual information and fine-grained local textures to alleviate feature degradation caused by scale variation. AWEM further enhances texture preservation and enhances discriminative capability via adaptive feature reweighting. In addition, MSAF improves multi-scale feature fusion by incorporating attention mechanisms, which enhance feature consistency while suppressing background interference and reducing misclassification in complex scenes.Extensive experiments conducted on multiple public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms several state-of-the-art detectors, particularly in small object detection tasks. Notably, RTP-Net achieves 84.1% mAP on the DIOR dataset, 95.3% mAP on the NWPU VHR-10 dataset, and 40.3% mAP on the AI-TOD dataset. These results validate the robustness and generalization capability of the proposed framework across diverse datasets and varying object scales.

item.title: An Adaptive Spatial-Frequency Interaction Fusion Framework with Competitive Distillation Strategy for Optical-SAR Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Ruining Liu
Ruiqi Wang
Ziquan Wang
Ling Guan
Meng Wu
Fengbin Zhang
Xinzheng Zhang
Dong Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Optical-Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) object detection can obtain robust and high-precision performance by integrating the rich semantic information in optical imagery with the stable structural information in SAR imagery. However, harsh weather conditions and electromagnetic interference environments often lead to a rapid degradation in imaging quality or even data loss, which inevitably imposes a formidable challenge on achieving robust cross-modal feature fusion. To alleviate this issue, an adaptive spatial-frequency interaction fusion framework with competitive distillation (ASIF-CD) is proposed in this paper. First, to mitigate the distribution differences between optical and SAR modalities, the adaptive spatial-frequency interaction (ASFI) module is developed to achieve cross-modal feature alignment and simultaneously suppress background noise. And then, the dynamic contribution fusion (DCF) module, which balances the contributions of optical spectral information and SAR structural features, is adopted to adjust feature fusion weights. Subsequently, a multi-modal competitive calibration distillation (MCCD) strategy is designed, which constructs a dynamic competitive optimization loss function to guide the fusion branch in achieving knowledge learning and mitigating the degradation of fused features. Finally, extensive experiments on the OGSOD and M4-SAR public datasets demonstrate that ASIF-CD yields m A P 50 m{mAP}_{50} scores of 92.6% and 86.0%, respectively, which consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art multi-modal fusion object detection methods. The code will be available at: https://github.com/lraining02/ASIF-CD. Published: 2026-07-21T19:04:32+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ruining Liu; Ruiqi Wang; Ziquan Wang; Ling Guan; Meng Wu; Fengbin Zhang; Xinzheng Zhang; Dong Li

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3715636

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Optical-Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) object detection can obtain robust and high-precision performance by integrating the rich semantic information in optical imagery with the stable structural information in SAR imagery. However, harsh weather conditions and electromagnetic interference environments often lead to a rapid degradation in imaging quality or even data loss, which inevitably imposes a formidable challenge on achieving robust cross-modal feature fusion. To alleviate this issue, an adaptive spatial-frequency interaction fusion framework with competitive distillation (ASIF-CD) is proposed in this paper. First, to mitigate the distribution differences between optical and SAR modalities, the adaptive spatial-frequency interaction (ASFI) module is developed to achieve cross-modal feature alignment and simultaneously suppress background noise. And then, the dynamic contribution fusion (DCF) module, which balances the contributions of optical spectral information and SAR structural features, is adopted to adjust feature fusion weights. Subsequently, a multi-modal competitive calibration distillation (MCCD) strategy is designed, which constructs a dynamic competitive optimization loss function to guide the fusion branch in achieving knowledge learning and mitigating the degradation of fused features. Finally, extensive experiments on the OGSOD and M4-SAR public datasets demonstrate that ASIF-CD yields m A P 50 m{mAP}_{50} scores of 92.6% and 86.0%, respectively, which consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art multi-modal fusion object detection methods. The code will be available at: https://github.com/lraining02/ASIF-CD.

item.title: SAR Image Object Detection Based on Cascaded Denoising and Counterfactual Self-Distillation
item.dc:creator: Luyun Tian
Yinju Nie
Guangjun He
Jie Geng
Wen Jiang
Shichao Jin
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Background noise in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images can severely degrade object detection performance. Although many methods employ complex image denoising strategies, target features in SAR imagery are tightly coupled with background noise. Meanwhile, excessive pixel-level smoothing inevitably weakens critical scattering details, resulting in the loss of discriminative and localization-relevant information. To address the above challenges, a Cascaded Denoising and Counterfactual Self-Distillation framework for SAR object detection (CDC-DETR) is proposed. By jointly optimizing multiple components of the detection model, CDC-DETR builds a multi-stage denoising architecture that suppresses noise while preserving critical target details. Specifically, a frequency-domain feature fusion module is introduced, which adaptively integrates features from different frequency bands through a gated mechanism. In addition, a background-aware encoder denoising module is designed to compute feature statistics guided by target semantic priors, enabling weighted suppression of interference from different background regions. This design encourages the model to focus on target areas while preserving relevant contextual information. Furthermore, to enhance discriminative target feature extraction, CDC-DETR incorporates a counterfactual self-distillation mechanism that derives counterfactual information from deformable attention maps and guides self-correction during training, thereby preventing the loss of semantic cues in background–target coupled regions. Extensive experiments on MSAR and CETC38-SAR datasets demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed method when compared with state-of-the-art techniques. Published: 2026-07-21T19:01:44+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Luyun Tian; Yinju Nie; Guangjun He; Jie Geng; Wen Jiang; Shichao Jin

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3715503

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Background noise in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images can severely degrade object detection performance. Although many methods employ complex image denoising strategies, target features in SAR imagery are tightly coupled with background noise. Meanwhile, excessive pixel-level smoothing inevitably weakens critical scattering details, resulting in the loss of discriminative and localization-relevant information. To address the above challenges, a Cascaded Denoising and Counterfactual Self-Distillation framework for SAR object detection (CDC-DETR) is proposed. By jointly optimizing multiple components of the detection model, CDC-DETR builds a multi-stage denoising architecture that suppresses noise while preserving critical target details. Specifically, a frequency-domain feature fusion module is introduced, which adaptively integrates features from different frequency bands through a gated mechanism. In addition, a background-aware encoder denoising module is designed to compute feature statistics guided by target semantic priors, enabling weighted suppression of interference from different background regions. This design encourages the model to focus on target areas while preserving relevant contextual information. Furthermore, to enhance discriminative target feature extraction, CDC-DETR incorporates a counterfactual self-distillation mechanism that derives counterfactual information from deformable attention maps and guides self-correction during training, thereby preventing the loss of semantic cues in background–target coupled regions. Extensive experiments on MSAR and CETC38-SAR datasets demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed method when compared with state-of-the-art techniques.

item.title: Neural sampling from cognitive maps enables goal-directed imagination and planning
item.dc:creator: Hui Lin
Yukun Yang
Rong Zhao
Giovanni Pezzulo
Wolfgang Maass
item.prism:publicationName: Nature Machine Intelligence
item.description: Abstract Artificial intelligence systems are becoming more intelligent, but at a very high cost in terms of energy consumption and training requirements. By contrast, our brains only require 20 W of energy, they learn online and they can instantly adjust to changing contingencies. This begs the question what data structures, algorithms and learning methods enable brains to achieve that, and whether these can be ported into artificial devices. We are addressing this question for a core feature of intelligence: the capacity to plan and solve problems, including new problems that involve states that were never encountered before. Here we examine three tools that brains are likely to use for achieving that: cognitive maps, stochastic computing and compositional coding. We integrate these tools into a transparent neural network model, and demonstrate its power for flexible planning and problem-solving. Importantly, this approach is suitable for implementation by in-memory computing and other energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware. In particular, it only requires self-supervised local synaptic plasticity that is suited for on-chip learning. Hence, a core feature of brain intelligence—the capacity to generate solutions to problems that were never encountered before—does not require deep neural networks or large language models, and can be implemented in energy-efficient edge devices. Published: 2026-07-21T15:00:58+00:00 Venue: Nature Machine Intelligence Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hui Lin; Yukun Yang; Rong Zhao; Giovanni Pezzulo; Wolfgang Maass

Journal: Nature Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1038/s42256-026-01254-4

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Abstract Artificial intelligence systems are becoming more intelligent, but at a very high cost in terms of energy consumption and training requirements. By contrast, our brains only require 20 W of energy, they learn online and they can instantly adjust to changing contingencies. This begs the question what data structures, algorithms and learning methods enable brains to achieve that, and whether these can be ported into artificial devices. We are addressing this question for a core feature of intelligence: the capacity to plan and solve problems, including new problems that involve states that were never encountered before. Here we examine three tools that brains are likely to use for achieving that: cognitive maps, stochastic computing and compositional coding. We integrate these tools into a transparent neural network model, and demonstrate its power for flexible planning and problem-solving. Importantly, this approach is suitable for implementation by in-memory computing and other energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware. In particular, it only requires self-supervised local synaptic plasticity that is suited for on-chip learning. Hence, a core feature of brain intelligence—the capacity to generate solutions to problems that were never encountered before—does not require deep neural networks or large language models, and can be implemented in energy-efficient edge devices.

item.title: Spectral-Spatial Synergistic Guided Network for Hyperspectral Salient Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Yanyan Peng
Tingfa Xu
Yao Xiao
Peifu Liu
Shuyan Bai
Fengxiang Xu
Jianan Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
item.description: Hyperspectral salient object detection aims to identify visually salient regions from hyperspectral images. Existing methods often fail because they fundamentally misunderstand the data, confusing incidental spectral variations caused by external factors such as illumination with essential spectral differences caused by the intrinsic material properties of the object. This leads to fragile representations and noisy predictions. To this end, we propose a lightweight and efficient Spectral-Spatial Synergistic Guided Network (S3GNet), with structure perception as the core, to build a closed-loop information flow around spectrum robust modeling, cross-stream co-perception and multi-scale refinement decoding. S3GNet introduces a parameter-free Spectral Structure-Aware Module that leverages spectral derivatives and regional hierarchical modeling to extract intrinsic features of robustness against illumination variations. Our Stream-Aware Attention Module achieves effective spectral-spatial collaboration through inter-stream global interaction and intra-stream spatial guidance. Furthermore, a Progressive Gated Refinement Decoder ensures precise object boundaries and detail recovery by optimally integrating multi-scale features. Experimental results show that S3GNet achieves superior performance in both computational efficiency and detection accuracy compared to existing methods. Published: 2026-07-22T19:08:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanyan Peng; Tingfa Xu; Yao Xiao; Peifu Liu; Shuyan Bai; Fengxiang Xu; Jianan Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3716067

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Hyperspectral salient object detection aims to identify visually salient regions from hyperspectral images. Existing methods often fail because they fundamentally misunderstand the data, confusing incidental spectral variations caused by external factors such as illumination with essential spectral differences caused by the intrinsic material properties of the object. This leads to fragile representations and noisy predictions. To this end, we propose a lightweight and efficient Spectral-Spatial Synergistic Guided Network (S3GNet), with structure perception as the core, to build a closed-loop information flow around spectrum robust modeling, cross-stream co-perception and multi-scale refinement decoding. S3GNet introduces a parameter-free Spectral Structure-Aware Module that leverages spectral derivatives and regional hierarchical modeling to extract intrinsic features of robustness against illumination variations. Our Stream-Aware Attention Module achieves effective spectral-spatial collaboration through inter-stream global interaction and intra-stream spatial guidance. Furthermore, a Progressive Gated Refinement Decoder ensures precise object boundaries and detail recovery by optimally integrating multi-scale features. Experimental results show that S3GNet achieves superior performance in both computational efficiency and detection accuracy compared to existing methods.

item.title: VCF-CLIP: Visual Context-Driven Fine-Grained Prompt Learning for Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection
item.dc:creator: Kaiwen Fu
Fei Qi
Chengyuan Chang
Jiahui Chen
Zhifu Zhao
Xiaotian Wang
Kun Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: Benefiting from recent advances in vision-language models (VLMs), numerous CLIP-based zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) methods have been proposed to address the cold-start problem. Despite their impressive performance, these methods still depend on manual prompt engineering, and their coarse-grained text prompts struggle to capture the diverse patterns of anomalies, resulting in suboptimal visual-text alignment. To overcome these limitations, we propose VCF-CLIP, a visual context-driven fine-grained prompt learning framework built upon CLIP. The novelties of VCF-CLIP lie in two main aspects. First, we propose the prompt prototype learning (PPL) strategy, which learns a pair of unified prompt prototypes representing general normal and anomalous states in a loss-guided manner, thereby eliminating the need for manual prompt design. Second, we propose a lightweight prompt refinement adapter that dynamically aggregates multiscale and multilevel visual features to iteratively refine the prompt prototypes, enabling the generation of instance-specific prompts enriched with fine-grained information. We conduct extensive experiments on 14 benchmarks across industrial and medical domains, and show that VCF-CLIP outperforms existing state-of-the-art ZSAD methods. Published: 2026-07-21T19:03:31+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Score: 0.796 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Kaiwen Fu; Fei Qi; Chengyuan Chang; Jiahui Chen; Zhifu Zhao; Xiaotian Wang; Kun Liu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tnnls.2026.3713374

Relevance: 0.796 (consider)

Benefiting from recent advances in vision-language models (VLMs), numerous CLIP-based zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) methods have been proposed to address the cold-start problem. Despite their impressive performance, these methods still depend on manual prompt engineering, and their coarse-grained text prompts struggle to capture the diverse patterns of anomalies, resulting in suboptimal visual-text alignment. To overcome these limitations, we propose VCF-CLIP, a visual context-driven fine-grained prompt learning framework built upon CLIP. The novelties of VCF-CLIP lie in two main aspects. First, we propose the prompt prototype learning (PPL) strategy, which learns a pair of unified prompt prototypes representing general normal and anomalous states in a loss-guided manner, thereby eliminating the need for manual prompt design. Second, we propose a lightweight prompt refinement adapter that dynamically aggregates multiscale and multilevel visual features to iteratively refine the prompt prototypes, enabling the generation of instance-specific prompts enriched with fine-grained information. We conduct extensive experiments on 14 benchmarks across industrial and medical domains, and show that VCF-CLIP outperforms existing state-of-the-art ZSAD methods.

item.title: Scattering Center Prior-Guided Diffusion for Unknown-Azimuth SAR Image Generation
item.dc:creator: Bingyu Han
Mou Wang
Shunjun Wei
Kun Chen
Zeyang Dai
Jin Li
Xiaowo Xu
Xiaoling Zhang
Zongyong Cui
Di Jiang
Yuanyuan Zhou
Pengcheng Gao
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: Generating synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images at unknown azimuth angles under limited sample conditions remains a challenging task, since target scattering characteristics vary significantly with observation angle and are difficult to model effectively using only image-domain priors. To address this issue, this paper proposes a scattering center prior-guided conditional diffusion framework for unknown-azimuth SAR image generation. First, the Iterative Shrinkage–Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA) constrained by the Point Spread Function (PSF) is employed to extract dominant scattering centers from SAR images at known viewing angles, obtaining sparse and physically interpretable scattering center maps. Subsequently, the target category, azimuth angle, and scattering map are jointly used as conditional vector inputs to train the diffusion model. During the inference stage, scattering maps from known azimuth angles are fused to construct a scattering prior for the unknown azimuth, which is used to guide the generation of the corresponding SAR image. Experimental results under sparse angular sampling conditions demonstrate that, compared with the scattering-guided GAN baseline and the non-learning image-domain interpolation baseline, the proposed method better preserves dominant scattering structures and generates unknown-azimuth SAR images with clearer target contours, more stable strong scattering regions, and fewer local artifacts. In summary, introducing dominant scattering center priors into the conditional diffusion model provides effective physical constraints for SAR image generation and improves unseen-azimuth SAR image generation under the evaluated sparse angular sampling conditions. Published: 2026-07-21T08:14:27+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.795 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Bingyu Han; Mou Wang; Shunjun Wei; Kun Chen; Zeyang Dai; Jin Li; Xiaowo Xu; Xiaoling Zhang; Zongyong Cui; Di Jiang; Yuanyuan Zhou; Pengcheng Gao

Journal: Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.3390/rs18142417

Relevance: 0.795 (consider)

Generating synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images at unknown azimuth angles under limited sample conditions remains a challenging task, since target scattering characteristics vary significantly with observation angle and are difficult to model effectively using only image-domain priors. To address this issue, this paper proposes a scattering center prior-guided conditional diffusion framework for unknown-azimuth SAR image generation. First, the Iterative Shrinkage–Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA) constrained by the Point Spread Function (PSF) is employed to extract dominant scattering centers from SAR images at known viewing angles, obtaining sparse and physically interpretable scattering center maps. Subsequently, the target category, azimuth angle, and scattering map are jointly used as conditional vector inputs to train the diffusion model. During the inference stage, scattering maps from known azimuth angles are fused to construct a scattering prior for the unknown azimuth, which is used to guide the generation of the corresponding SAR image. Experimental results under sparse angular sampling conditions demonstrate that, compared with the scattering-guided GAN baseline and the non-learning image-domain interpolation baseline, the proposed method better preserves dominant scattering structures and generates unknown-azimuth SAR images with clearer target contours, more stable strong scattering regions, and fewer local artifacts. In summary, introducing dominant scattering center priors into the conditional diffusion model provides effective physical constraints for SAR image generation and improves unseen-azimuth SAR image generation under the evaluated sparse angular sampling conditions.

item.title: Adversarial Robustness in Zero-Shot Learning: An Empirical Study on Class and Concept-Level Vulnerabilities
item.dc:creator: Zihan Ye
Zhiyuan Peng
Shreyank N Gowda
Yuping Yan
Haotian Xu
Ling Shao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Zero-shot Learning (ZSL) aims to enable image classifiers to recognize images from unseen classes that were not included during training. Unlike traditional supervised classification, ZSL typically relies on learning a mapping from visual features to predefined, human-understandable class concepts. While ZSL models promise to improve generalization and interpretability, their robustness under systematic input perturbations remain unclear. In this study, we present an empirical analysis about the robustness of existing ZSL methods at both class-level and concept-level. Specifically, we successfully disrupted their class prediction by the well-known non-target class attack (clsA). However, in the Generalized Zero-shot Learning (GZSL) setting, we observe that the success of clsA is only at the original best-calibrated point. After the attack, the optimal best-calibration point shifts, and ZSL models maintain relatively strong performance at other calibration points, indicating that clsA results in a spurious attack success in the GZSL. To address this, we propose the Class-Bias Enhanced Attack (CBEA), which completely eliminates GZSL accuracy across all calibrated points by enhancing the gap between seen and unseen class probabilities. Next, at concept-level attack, we introduce two novel attack modes: Class-Preserving Concept Attack (CPconA) and Non-Class-Preserving Concept Attack (NCPconA). Our extensive experiments evaluate three typical ZSL models across various architectures from the past three years and reveal that ZSL models are vulnerable not only to the traditional class attack but also to concept-based attacks. These attacks allow malicious actors to easily manipulate class predictions by erasing or introducing concepts. Our findings highlight a significant performance gap between existing approaches, emphasizing the need for improved adversarial robustness in current ZSL models. Our codes are available at https://github.com/FouriYe/AttackZSL TIP26. Published: 2026-07-21T19:01:40+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.788 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zihan Ye; Zhiyuan Peng; Shreyank N Gowda; Yuping Yan; Haotian Xu; Ling Shao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3709493

Relevance: 0.788 (consider)

Zero-shot Learning (ZSL) aims to enable image classifiers to recognize images from unseen classes that were not included during training. Unlike traditional supervised classification, ZSL typically relies on learning a mapping from visual features to predefined, human-understandable class concepts. While ZSL models promise to improve generalization and interpretability, their robustness under systematic input perturbations remain unclear. In this study, we present an empirical analysis about the robustness of existing ZSL methods at both class-level and concept-level. Specifically, we successfully disrupted their class prediction by the well-known non-target class attack (clsA). However, in the Generalized Zero-shot Learning (GZSL) setting, we observe that the success of clsA is only at the original best-calibrated point. After the attack, the optimal best-calibration point shifts, and ZSL models maintain relatively strong performance at other calibration points, indicating that clsA results in a spurious attack success in the GZSL. To address this, we propose the Class-Bias Enhanced Attack (CBEA), which completely eliminates GZSL accuracy across all calibrated points by enhancing the gap between seen and unseen class probabilities. Next, at concept-level attack, we introduce two novel attack modes: Class-Preserving Concept Attack (CPconA) and Non-Class-Preserving Concept Attack (NCPconA). Our extensive experiments evaluate three typical ZSL models across various architectures from the past three years and reveal that ZSL models are vulnerable not only to the traditional class attack but also to concept-based attacks. These attacks allow malicious actors to easily manipulate class predictions by erasing or introducing concepts. Our findings highlight a significant performance gap between existing approaches, emphasizing the need for improved adversarial robustness in current ZSL models. Our codes are available at https://github.com/FouriYe/AttackZSL TIP26.

item.title: Cross-View Change Detection via Self-Supervised Learning
item.dc:creator: Ghassen Graja
Olfa Besbes
Takoua Abdellatif
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: This paper addresses the problem of cross-view heterogeneous change detection using satellite images and unmanned aerial vehicle imagery, a setting characterized by severe viewpoint differences, scale variations, and sensing modality discrepancies, as well as the absence of reliable labels. To overcome these challenges, we propose a self-supervised contrastive and predictive learning framework that learns discriminative representations directly from unlabeled cross-view image pairs. The framework jointly exploits patch-level and pixel-level objectives to capture both fine-grained local changes and higher-level semantic consistency across views. In addition, a CutMix-based data augmentation strategy is introduced to improve representation diversity. We further enhance Swin Transformer with cross-attention and channel attention mechanisms to facilitate effective multi-scale and cross-modal feature interaction. Experimental results on cross-view and bi-temporal change detection datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves robust and competitive performance without relying on precise geometric alignment or manual annotations, highlighting its practical relevance for real-world Earth observation applications involving heterogeneous and rapidly acquired data. The PyTorch implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/ghassengraja/SS-SwinX. Published: 2026-07-21T19:03:21+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.784 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ghassen Graja; Olfa Besbes; Takoua Abdellatif

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3715872

Relevance: 0.784 (consider)

This paper addresses the problem of cross-view heterogeneous change detection using satellite images and unmanned aerial vehicle imagery, a setting characterized by severe viewpoint differences, scale variations, and sensing modality discrepancies, as well as the absence of reliable labels. To overcome these challenges, we propose a self-supervised contrastive and predictive learning framework that learns discriminative representations directly from unlabeled cross-view image pairs. The framework jointly exploits patch-level and pixel-level objectives to capture both fine-grained local changes and higher-level semantic consistency across views. In addition, a CutMix-based data augmentation strategy is introduced to improve representation diversity. We further enhance Swin Transformer with cross-attention and channel attention mechanisms to facilitate effective multi-scale and cross-modal feature interaction. Experimental results on cross-view and bi-temporal change detection datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves robust and competitive performance without relying on precise geometric alignment or manual annotations, highlighting its practical relevance for real-world Earth observation applications involving heterogeneous and rapidly acquired data. The PyTorch implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/ghassengraja/SS-SwinX.

item.title: MRM++: Enhanced Masked Relation Modeling for Multi-Modal Medical Pre-training
item.dc:creator: Qiushi Yang
Wuyang Li
Zhe Peng
Fangxiao Cheng
Yixuan Yuan
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Computer Vision
item.description: Abstract Recent progress in deep learning for automated multi-modal medical diagnosis heavily depend on extensive expert annotations, which is time-intensive and impractical. To mitigate this, masked image modeling (MIM)-based pre-training strategies have emerged, effectively learning generalized representations from unlabelled data for various downstream tasks. Nevertheless, these approaches are tailored for natural images while neglect the distinct characteristics of medical data, resulting in suboptimal generalization in medical diagnosis applications. In this work, we attempt to harness the complementary information of multi-modal medical data to perform self-supervised pre-training and propose MRM++, an enhanced masked relation modeling paradigm. Different from the previous MIM methods that randomly mask input data, causing potentially missing of disease-relevant semantics, we devise prior-guided relation masking to break token-wise feature relation guided by anatomy-aware prior in both self- and cross-modal aspects. This can preserve complete input semantics and enable the model to learn abundant disease-related knowledge. Furthermore, to boost semantic relation modeling, the relation matching is introduced, which aligns sample-wise relations among unmasked and masked features. By exploiting inter-sample relations, the relation matching imposes the global constraints in the feature space, ensuring ample semantic relation for robust feature representation. Additionally, considering that the model may overfit to the pre-training dataset and lead to inherent gap between pre-training and downstream fine-tuning, we conceive task-oriented adapting as a pre-stage before fine-tuning to simultaneously perform self-supervised and task-supervised learning on downstream dataset. It can adaptively transform knowledge from the pre-trained model to be compatible with downstream tasks while maintaining transferable information. Extensive experiments on medical image-text and image-genome benchmarks validate the effectiveness and transfer ability of the proposed framework, outperforming state-of-the-art methods across various downstream diagnostic tasks. Source codes are made publicly available on https://github.com/CUHK-AIM-Group/MRM_plus . Published: 2026-07-21T05:32:10+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.783 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qiushi Yang; Wuyang Li; Zhe Peng; Fangxiao Cheng; Yixuan Yuan

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02938-5

Relevance: 0.783 (consider)

Abstract Recent progress in deep learning for automated multi-modal medical diagnosis heavily depend on extensive expert annotations, which is time-intensive and impractical. To mitigate this, masked image modeling (MIM)-based pre-training strategies have emerged, effectively learning generalized representations from unlabelled data for various downstream tasks. Nevertheless, these approaches are tailored for natural images while neglect the distinct characteristics of medical data, resulting in suboptimal generalization in medical diagnosis applications. In this work, we attempt to harness the complementary information of multi-modal medical data to perform self-supervised pre-training and propose MRM++, an enhanced masked relation modeling paradigm. Different from the previous MIM methods that randomly mask input data, causing potentially missing of disease-relevant semantics, we devise prior-guided relation masking to break token-wise feature relation guided by anatomy-aware prior in both self- and cross-modal aspects. This can preserve complete input semantics and enable the model to learn abundant disease-related knowledge. Furthermore, to boost semantic relation modeling, the relation matching is introduced, which aligns sample-wise relations among unmasked and masked features. By exploiting inter-sample relations, the relation matching imposes the global constraints in the feature space, ensuring ample semantic relation for robust feature representation. Additionally, considering that the model may overfit to the pre-training dataset and lead to inherent gap between pre-training and downstream fine-tuning, we conceive task-oriented adapting as a pre-stage before fine-tuning to simultaneously perform self-supervised and task-supervised learning on downstream dataset. It can adaptively transform knowledge from the pre-trained model to be compatible with downstream tasks while maintaining transferable information. Extensive experiments on medical image-text and image-genome benchmarks validate the effectiveness and transfer ability of the proposed framework, outperforming state-of-the-art methods across various downstream diagnostic tasks. Source codes are made publicly available on https://github.com/CUHK-AIM-Group/MRM_plus .

item.title: A Lightweight Mamba Network for the Joint Classification of Hyperspectral and LiDAR Data
item.dc:creator: Diling Liao
Junyi Liu
Cuilin Yu
Qingsong Wang
Tao Lai
Haifeng Huang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: To address the high computational complexity inherent in the joint classification of hyperspectral image (HSI) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data, along with the limitation of existing Mamba-based scanning mechanisms in adequately capturing spatial context, this paper proposes a lightweight multimodal Mamba network, termed LWM-Mamba. The proposed network first employs a shallow feature embedding module to extract initial features from HSI and LiDAR data, thereby mitigating the computational burden arising from spectral redundancy in HSI. Second, a frequency enhancement module (FEM) is designed to augment local and global features as well as fine-grained details, while a spatial compensation mechanism is introduced to preserve feature integrity. Subsequently, a multimodal adaptive Mamba (MA-Mamba) module is constructed. This module first adopts a multi-path scanning mechanism that initiates scanning from the central pixel along multiple directions, including rotational and inverse rotational paths, to comprehensively capture spatial dependencies. Furthermore, the module incorporates an adaptive state space model (SSM) to facilitate deep interaction and dynamic integration of cross-modal information. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed network achieves superior classification performance compared with state-of-the-art methods while significantly reducing computational complexity, thereby validating its efficiency and robustness in HSI–LiDAR joint classification tasks. The source code of LWM-Mamba will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Dilingliao/LWM-Mamba. Published: 2026-07-22T19:06:50+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.783 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Diling Liao; Junyi Liu; Cuilin Yu; Qingsong Wang; Tao Lai; Haifeng Huang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3715923

Relevance: 0.783 (consider)

To address the high computational complexity inherent in the joint classification of hyperspectral image (HSI) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data, along with the limitation of existing Mamba-based scanning mechanisms in adequately capturing spatial context, this paper proposes a lightweight multimodal Mamba network, termed LWM-Mamba. The proposed network first employs a shallow feature embedding module to extract initial features from HSI and LiDAR data, thereby mitigating the computational burden arising from spectral redundancy in HSI. Second, a frequency enhancement module (FEM) is designed to augment local and global features as well as fine-grained details, while a spatial compensation mechanism is introduced to preserve feature integrity. Subsequently, a multimodal adaptive Mamba (MA-Mamba) module is constructed. This module first adopts a multi-path scanning mechanism that initiates scanning from the central pixel along multiple directions, including rotational and inverse rotational paths, to comprehensively capture spatial dependencies. Furthermore, the module incorporates an adaptive state space model (SSM) to facilitate deep interaction and dynamic integration of cross-modal information. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed network achieves superior classification performance compared with state-of-the-art methods while significantly reducing computational complexity, thereby validating its efficiency and robustness in HSI–LiDAR joint classification tasks. The source code of LWM-Mamba will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Dilingliao/LWM-Mamba.

item.title: 平行图像：从理论框架到多模态智能视觉应用
item.dc:creator: Zhang Hui
Tian Yonglin
Wang Yutong
Gou Chao
Li Xuan
Wang Feiyue
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Image and Graphics
item.description: 在深度学习推动下，视觉感知系统在智能驾驶、安防监控和医疗诊断等领域取得显著进展。然而，现实场景中数据分布的极度不均衡、长尾样本的稀缺性及高昂的人工标注成本，已成为制约模型性能与泛化能力的关键瓶颈。平行图像作为基于平行系统理论发展而来的新型图像生成与建模方法体系，通过构建人工场景系统、开展计算实验推演及虚实平行执行，形成“建模—训练—反馈—优化”的闭环机制，为视觉感知系统提供了高质量、多样化、结构化的合成数据支撑。本文系统梳理了平行图像的理论基础与发展脉络，重点综述其在虚拟场景生成、多模态特征融合、虚实域迁移和异构知识驱动的平行推理等关键技术路径上的研究进展与应用探索。同时，结合当前生成式人工智能和大规模多模态基础模型的发展趋势，分析了平行图像在智能视觉系统演化中的融合潜力。最后，本文指出该领域面临的主要挑战，并对未来的研究方向和应用前景进行了展望。 Published: 2026-07-21T00:55:25+00:00 Venue: Journal of Image and Graphics Score: 0.779 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhang Hui; Tian Yonglin; Wang Yutong; Gou Chao; Li Xuan; Wang Feiyue

Journal: Journal of Image and Graphics

DOI: 10.11834/jig.250231

在深度学习推动下，视觉感知系统在智能驾驶、安防监控和医疗诊断等领域取得显著进展。然而，现实场景中数据分布的极度不均衡、长尾样本的稀缺性及高昂的人工标注成本，已成为制约模型性能与泛化能力的关键瓶颈。平行图像作为基于平行系统理论发展而来的新型图像生成与建模方法体系，通过构建人工场景系统、开展计算实验推演及虚实平行执行，形成“建模—训练—反馈—优化”的闭环机制，为视觉感知系统提供了高质量、多样化、结构化的合成数据支撑。本文系统梳理了平行图像的理论基础与发展脉络，重点综述其在虚拟场景生成、多模态特征融合、虚实域迁移和异构知识驱动的平行推理等关键技术路径上的研究进展与应用探索。同时，结合当前生成式人工智能和大规模多模态基础模型的发展趋势，分析了平行图像在智能视觉系统演化中的融合潜力。最后，本文指出该领域面临的主要挑战，并对未来的研究方向和应用前景进行了展望。

item.title: DCA-YOLO: A Dynamic Cross-Attention Network for Small Vehicle Detection in UAV Aerial Imagery
item.dc:creator: Chaojun Dong
Yizhi Wang
Yikui Zhai
Ye Li
Xiankun Liu
Chaoyun Mai
Jianhong Zhou
Pasquale Coscia
Angelo Genovese
C. L. Philip Chen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: To address challenges in UAV aerial imagery, such as complex backgrounds, drastic scale variations, and partial occlusions. We propose DCA-YOLO, a high-performance vehicle detection framework featuring a newly designed neck architecture named the dynamic cross-attention path aggregation network (DCA-PAN). Our approach introduces three synergistic modules in the neck to enhance feature expressiveness for small vehicle detection: dynamic weighted adaptive feature fusion, cross-layer dynamic interaction, and coordinate-aware dynamic attention. These modules synergistically improve multi-scale representation, seamlessly integrate shallow details with deep semantics, and dynamically emphasize key object features. To this end, we leverage the efficient and powerful FasterNet as the backbone to achieve an effective balance between computational overhead and detection accuracy. Experiments on the VisDrone and UAV-HS datasets demonstrate that DCA-YOLO achieves an optimal accuracy-efficiency tradeoff among compact detectors. On the challenging VisDrone dataset, DCA-YOLO significantly outperforms the baseline YOLOv8n, boosting mAP 50 _{50} from 57.2% to 60.9%, mAP 50 : 95 _{50:95} from 38.5% to 41.6%, and mAP s _{s} from 14.3% to 16.1%. Published: 2026-07-21T19:04:44+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.778 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chaojun Dong; Yizhi Wang; Yikui Zhai; Ye Li; Xiankun Liu; Chaoyun Mai; Jianhong Zhou; Pasquale Coscia; Angelo Genovese; C. L. Philip Chen

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3715596

Relevance: 0.778 (consider)

To address challenges in UAV aerial imagery, such as complex backgrounds, drastic scale variations, and partial occlusions. We propose DCA-YOLO, a high-performance vehicle detection framework featuring a newly designed neck architecture named the dynamic cross-attention path aggregation network (DCA-PAN). Our approach introduces three synergistic modules in the neck to enhance feature expressiveness for small vehicle detection: dynamic weighted adaptive feature fusion, cross-layer dynamic interaction, and coordinate-aware dynamic attention. These modules synergistically improve multi-scale representation, seamlessly integrate shallow details with deep semantics, and dynamically emphasize key object features. To this end, we leverage the efficient and powerful FasterNet as the backbone to achieve an effective balance between computational overhead and detection accuracy. Experiments on the VisDrone and UAV-HS datasets demonstrate that DCA-YOLO achieves an optimal accuracy-efficiency tradeoff among compact detectors. On the challenging VisDrone dataset, DCA-YOLO significantly outperforms the baseline YOLOv8n, boosting mAP 50 _{50} from 57.2% to 60.9%, mAP 50 : 95 _{50:95} from 38.5% to 41.6%, and mAP s _{s} from 14.3% to 16.1%.

item.title: Remote Sensing Lithology Interpretation via a Response-Optimized Structure-Aware Multimodal Fusion Network
item.dc:creator: Ruilong Han
Kang He
Ruyi Feng
Zhijun Zhang
Jie Dong
Yusen Dong
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Lithological interpretation, a fundamental task in geological remote sensing, aims to discriminate different rock and soil types, playing a crucial role in geological mapping, resource exploration, and environmental monitoring. Although deep learning techniques have shown superior capabilities in feature extraction and generalization, their application to lithological interpretation remains challenging. These challenges primarily arise from inconsistent responses in multimodal data, which lead to redundant information that degrades model performance. In addition, conventional convolutions show limited capability in mining lithological features during multimodal fusion. To overcome these challenges, we propose a response-optimized structure-aware multimodal fusion network (ROSANet) for lithological interpretation. First, we propose a responsiveness-aware cross-modal channel substitution (RCCS) module to identify and replace low-response channels between modalities based on activation and normalization statistics, thereby suppressing redundant information and enhancing salient features. Second, we propose a dynamic structure-aware fusion (DSF) module that fuses multimodal features through compression and reconstruction, integrating a learnable non-uniform receptive mechanism to capture lithological directional features. Furthermore, Sentinel-1 SAR data are incorporated as a complementary modality, providing scattering-based structural cues that compensate for the limitations in optical channels. Comprehensive experiments conducted on the Yingjisha dataset and the Djibouti dataset demonstrate that ROSANet achieves improvements of 2.7% in oPA and 4.2% in mIoU compared to eight state-of-the-art models. These results verify the effectiveness and robustness of ROSANet for lithological interpretation in complex geological environments. Published: 2026-07-21T19:03:49+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.775 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ruilong Han; Kang He; Ruyi Feng; Zhijun Zhang; Jie Dong; Yusen Dong

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3715685

Relevance: 0.775 (consider)

Lithological interpretation, a fundamental task in geological remote sensing, aims to discriminate different rock and soil types, playing a crucial role in geological mapping, resource exploration, and environmental monitoring. Although deep learning techniques have shown superior capabilities in feature extraction and generalization, their application to lithological interpretation remains challenging. These challenges primarily arise from inconsistent responses in multimodal data, which lead to redundant information that degrades model performance. In addition, conventional convolutions show limited capability in mining lithological features during multimodal fusion. To overcome these challenges, we propose a response-optimized structure-aware multimodal fusion network (ROSANet) for lithological interpretation. First, we propose a responsiveness-aware cross-modal channel substitution (RCCS) module to identify and replace low-response channels between modalities based on activation and normalization statistics, thereby suppressing redundant information and enhancing salient features. Second, we propose a dynamic structure-aware fusion (DSF) module that fuses multimodal features through compression and reconstruction, integrating a learnable non-uniform receptive mechanism to capture lithological directional features. Furthermore, Sentinel-1 SAR data are incorporated as a complementary modality, providing scattering-based structural cues that compensate for the limitations in optical channels. Comprehensive experiments conducted on the Yingjisha dataset and the Djibouti dataset demonstrate that ROSANet achieves improvements of 2.7% in oPA and 4.2% in mIoU compared to eight state-of-the-art models. These results verify the effectiveness and robustness of ROSANet for lithological interpretation in complex geological environments.

item.title: Dual-Domain Adversarial Purification for Robust Remote Sensing Scene Classification
item.dc:creator: Yuru Su
Shaohui Mei
Mingyang Ma
Shuai Wan
Jiao Shi
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Deep learning has boosted remote sensing (RS) scene classification, but adversarial examples can still cause high-confidence misclassification with imperceptible perturbations. Adversarial purification offers a practical test-time defense without retraining the classifier. However, most existing methods are confined to pixel-space restoration, which may leave residual adversarial effects that persist and amplify through feature extraction, ultimately biasing the prediction. To address these issues, a Dual-Domain Adversarial Purification (DDAP) framework is proposed to mitigate adversarial effects at both the pixel and feature levels in a unified pipeline. In the pixel domain, a Pixel-Domain Frequency-Aware Diffusion Purification (PFDP) module performs diffusion-based restoration through a Frequency-Aware Dual-Stream U-Net (FD-UNet). By integrating adaptive spectral filtering with multi-domain consistency constraints, PFDP reduces adversarial-perturbation-dominated high-frequency responses while preserving structural details and semantic information in RS imagery. In the feature domain, an Adversarial Vulnerable Channel Dropout (AVCD) strategy models unshifted shallow-feature statistics with a Gaussian Mixture Model and adaptively assigns channel-wise dropout probabilities based on a sample-wise shift score and channel vulnerability, thereby suppressing residual adversarial influence before downstream classification. Extensive experiments on UCM and AID across multiple backbones and attack types demonstrate that DDAP consistently improves robustness while maintaining a favorable clean–robust balance compared with representative baselines. Published: 2026-07-21T19:03:58+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.775 (consider)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

Relevance: 0.775 (consider)

Deep learning has boosted remote sensing (RS) scene classification, but adversarial examples can still cause high-confidence misclassification with imperceptible perturbations. Adversarial purification offers a practical test-time defense without retraining the classifier. However, most existing methods are confined to pixel-space restoration, which may leave residual adversarial effects that persist and amplify through feature extraction, ultimately biasing the prediction. To address these issues, a Dual-Domain Adversarial Purification (DDAP) framework is proposed to mitigate adversarial effects at both the pixel and feature levels in a unified pipeline. In the pixel domain, a Pixel-Domain Frequency-Aware Diffusion Purification (PFDP) module performs diffusion-based restoration through a Frequency-Aware Dual-Stream U-Net (FD-UNet). By integrating adaptive spectral filtering with multi-domain consistency constraints, PFDP reduces adversarial-perturbation-dominated high-frequency responses while preserving structural details and semantic information in RS imagery. In the feature domain, an Adversarial Vulnerable Channel Dropout (AVCD) strategy models unshifted shallow-feature statistics with a Gaussian Mixture Model and adaptively assigns channel-wise dropout probabilities based on a sample-wise shift score and channel vulnerability, thereby suppressing residual adversarial influence before downstream classification. Extensive experiments on UCM and AID across multiple backbones and attack types demonstrate that DDAP consistently improves robustness while maintaining a favorable clean–robust balance compared with representative baselines.

item.title: Chain-of-Thought for Autonomous Driving: A Comprehensive Survey and Future Prospects
item.dc:creator: Yixin Cui
Haotian Lin
Shuo Yang
Yixiao Wang
Yanjun Huang
Hong Chen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: The rapid evolution of large language models (LLMs) in natural language processing has substantially elevated their semantic understanding and logical reasoning capabilities. Such proficiencies have been leveraged in autonomous driving systems, contributing to significant improvements in system performance. Models, such as OpenAI o1 and DeepSeek-R1, leverage chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, an advanced cognitive method that simulates human thinking processes, demonstrating remarkable reasoning capabilities in complex tasks. By structuring complex driving scenarios within a systematic reasoning framework, this approach has emerged as a prominent research focus in autonomous driving, substantially improving the system’s ability to handle challenging cases. This article investigates how CoT methods improve the reasoning abilities of autonomous driving models. Based on a comprehensive literature review, we present a systematic analysis of the motivations, methodologies, challenges, and future research directions of CoT in autonomous driving. Furthermore, we propose the insight of combining CoT with self-learning to facilitate self-evolution in driving systems. To ensure the relevance and timeliness of this study, we have compiled a dynamic repository of literature and open-source projects, diligently updated to incorporate forefront developments. The repository is publicly available at https://github.com/cuiyx1720/Awesome-CoT4AD Published: 2026-07-21T19:02:04+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Score: 0.773 (consider)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

The rapid evolution of large language models (LLMs) in natural language processing has substantially elevated their semantic understanding and logical reasoning capabilities. Such proficiencies have been leveraged in autonomous driving systems, contributing to significant improvements in system performance. Models, such as OpenAI o1 and DeepSeek-R1, leverage chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, an advanced cognitive method that simulates human thinking processes, demonstrating remarkable reasoning capabilities in complex tasks. By structuring complex driving scenarios within a systematic reasoning framework, this approach has emerged as a prominent research focus in autonomous driving, substantially improving the system’s ability to handle challenging cases. This article investigates how CoT methods improve the reasoning abilities of autonomous driving models. Based on a comprehensive literature review, we present a systematic analysis of the motivations, methodologies, challenges, and future research directions of CoT in autonomous driving. Furthermore, we propose the insight of combining CoT with self-learning to facilitate self-evolution in driving systems. To ensure the relevance and timeliness of this study, we have compiled a dynamic repository of literature and open-source projects, diligently updated to incorporate forefront developments. The repository is publicly available at https://github.com/cuiyx1720/Awesome-CoT4AD

item.title: Mixture of Receptive Fields Fusion for Accurate Rice Panicle Segmentation and Yield Prediction in UAV-based Remote Sensing
item.dc:creator: Zibin Zhou
Xuan Wei
Huasheng Huang
Tao Guo
Jiajun Zhuang
Zhiping Tan
Jinchang Ren
Yu Tang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: UAV-based remote sensing enables high-throughput rice phenotyping, yet precise panicle segmentation remains challenging. Conventional plot-level vegetation indices or panicle counting proxies inherently discard pixel-level structural information, rendering them incapable of organ-level morphological analysis. While ultra-high-resolution cameras now enable fine-grained segmentation, three interrelated bottlenecks persist: extreme scale heterogeneity across genotypes induces receptive field mismatch, dense canopy occlusion coupled with spectral similarity between withered leaves and panicles causes severe foreground-background confusion, and gigapixel-scale orthomosaics for thousands of micro-plots impose prohibitive computational burdens. To address these, we propose Mixture of Receptive Fields Fusion (MiRF), where Dynamic Preview Convolution (DyPConv) adaptively selects optimal dilation rates to capture critical local contextual information across diverse genotypic panicle morphologies while compressing computation to only K active branches via hard path pruning; Frequency-Aware Refinement (FAR) leverages Laplacian high-frequency components with learnable gating to explicitly magnify textural disparities between panicles and cluttered backgrounds of withered leaves; and Efficient Cross-scale Feature Aggregator (ECFA) employs globally shared bidirectional fusion to reduce computational burdens when processing gigapixel-scale orthomosaics while preserving multi-scale context. Experiments on two public datasets achieve state-of-the-art IoU-Panicles (+3.48% and +3.11%) and F1-scores (+2.83% and +2.31%) with significantly reduced complexity. Cross-season validation on 610 varieties spanning 2024–2025 achieves R² exceeding 0.826 for yield prediction, supporting MiRF's generalization across diverse genotypes. Edge deployment experiments demonstrate its superiority in real-time processing. Published: 2026-07-22T19:06:56+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.770 (consider)

Authors: Zibin Zhou; Xuan Wei; Huasheng Huang; Tao Guo; Jiajun Zhuang; Zhiping Tan; Jinchang Ren; Yu Tang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3715962

Relevance: 0.770 (consider)

UAV-based remote sensing enables high-throughput rice phenotyping, yet precise panicle segmentation remains challenging. Conventional plot-level vegetation indices or panicle counting proxies inherently discard pixel-level structural information, rendering them incapable of organ-level morphological analysis. While ultra-high-resolution cameras now enable fine-grained segmentation, three interrelated bottlenecks persist: extreme scale heterogeneity across genotypes induces receptive field mismatch, dense canopy occlusion coupled with spectral similarity between withered leaves and panicles causes severe foreground-background confusion, and gigapixel-scale orthomosaics for thousands of micro-plots impose prohibitive computational burdens. To address these, we propose Mixture of Receptive Fields Fusion (MiRF), where Dynamic Preview Convolution (DyPConv) adaptively selects optimal dilation rates to capture critical local contextual information across diverse genotypic panicle morphologies while compressing computation to only K active branches via hard path pruning; Frequency-Aware Refinement (FAR) leverages Laplacian high-frequency components with learnable gating to explicitly magnify textural disparities between panicles and cluttered backgrounds of withered leaves; and Efficient Cross-scale Feature Aggregator (ECFA) employs globally shared bidirectional fusion to reduce computational burdens when processing gigapixel-scale orthomosaics while preserving multi-scale context. Experiments on two public datasets achieve state-of-the-art IoU-Panicles (+3.48% and +3.11%) and F1-scores (+2.83% and +2.31%) with significantly reduced complexity. Cross-season validation on 610 varieties spanning 2024–2025 achieves R² exceeding 0.826 for yield prediction, supporting MiRF's generalization across diverse genotypes. Edge deployment experiments demonstrate its superiority in real-time processing.

item.title: Kolmogorov-Arnold Network for Remote Sensing Image Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Ziyao Wang
Yin Hu
Xiaokang Zhang
Yuanxin Ye
Xianping Ma
Man-On Pun
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Semantic segmentation is vital for remote sensing applications, where accurate feature extraction and representation are essential. Existing encoder-decoder architectures often fail to fully utilize high-dimensional features and recover fine details during decoding. To address this problem, we propose DeepKANSeg, a novel network based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN). KAN’s ability to decompose complex functions into univariate transformations enables flexible modeling of intricate data patterns. Our approach introduces two key innovations: a KAN-based deep feature refinement (DFR) module composed of DeepKAN to capture complex spatial and semantic relationships from high-dimensional features, and a global-local KAN (GLKAN) module replacing the traditional multi-layer perceptron (MLP) layers with KAN-based linear layers to enhance fine-grained decoding. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method, extensive experiments are conducted on two well-known fine-resolution remote sensing benchmark datasets, namely ISPRS Vaihingen and ISPRS Potsdam. The results demonstrate that the KAN-enhanced segmentation model achieves superior performance in terms of accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, the univariate decomposition improves interpretability, making it suitable for explainable learning in remote sensing. The source code for this work will be accessible at https://github.com/sstary/SSRS. Published: 2026-07-21T19:02:20+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.768 (consider)

Semantic segmentation is vital for remote sensing applications, where accurate feature extraction and representation are essential. Existing encoder-decoder architectures often fail to fully utilize high-dimensional features and recover fine details during decoding. To address this problem, we propose DeepKANSeg, a novel network based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN). KAN’s ability to decompose complex functions into univariate transformations enables flexible modeling of intricate data patterns. Our approach introduces two key innovations: a KAN-based deep feature refinement (DFR) module composed of DeepKAN to capture complex spatial and semantic relationships from high-dimensional features, and a global-local KAN (GLKAN) module replacing the traditional multi-layer perceptron (MLP) layers with KAN-based linear layers to enhance fine-grained decoding. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method, extensive experiments are conducted on two well-known fine-resolution remote sensing benchmark datasets, namely ISPRS Vaihingen and ISPRS Potsdam. The results demonstrate that the KAN-enhanced segmentation model achieves superior performance in terms of accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, the univariate decomposition improves interpretability, making it suitable for explainable learning in remote sensing. The source code for this work will be accessible at https://github.com/sstary/SSRS.

item.title: An Efficient Semantic Segmentation Transformer for In-Car or Distributed Applications
item.dc:creator: Danish Nazir
Gowtham Sai Inti
Timo Bartels
Jan Piewek
Thorsten Bagdonat
Tim Fingscheidt
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Modern automotive systems leverage deep neural networks (DNNs) for semantic segmentation and operate in two key application areas: (1) In-car, where the DNN solely operates in the vehicle without strict constraints on the data rate. (2) Distributed, where one DNN part operates in the vehicle and the other part typically on a large-scale cloud platform with a particular constraint on transmission bitrate efficiency. Typically, both applications share an image and source encoder, while each uses distinct (joint) source and task decoders. Prior work utilized convolutional neural networks for joint source and task decoding but did not investigate transformer-based alternatives such as SegDeformer, which offer superior performance at the cost of higher computational complexity. In this work, we propose joint feature and task decoding for SegDeformer, thereby enabling lower computational complexity in both in-car and distributed applications, despite SegDeformer’s computational demands. This improves scalability in the cloud while reducing in-car computational complexity. For the in-car application, we increased the frames per second (fps) by up to a factor of 11.7 (1.4 fps to 16.5 fps) on Cityscapes and by up to a factor of 3.5 (43.3 fps to 154.3 fps) on ADE20K, while being on-par w.r.t. the mean intersection over union (mIoU) of the transformer-based baseline that doesn’t compress by a source codec. For the distributed application, we achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) over a wide range of bitrates on the mIoU metric, while using only 0.14% (0.04%) of cloud DNN parameters used in previous SOTA, reported on ADE20K (Cityscapes). Published: 2026-07-21T19:10:19+00:00 Venue: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.766 (consider)

Modern automotive systems leverage deep neural networks (DNNs) for semantic segmentation and operate in two key application areas: (1) In-car, where the DNN solely operates in the vehicle without strict constraints on the data rate. (2) Distributed, where one DNN part operates in the vehicle and the other part typically on a large-scale cloud platform with a particular constraint on transmission bitrate efficiency. Typically, both applications share an image and source encoder, while each uses distinct (joint) source and task decoders. Prior work utilized convolutional neural networks for joint source and task decoding but did not investigate transformer-based alternatives such as SegDeformer, which offer superior performance at the cost of higher computational complexity. In this work, we propose joint feature and task decoding for SegDeformer, thereby enabling lower computational complexity in both in-car and distributed applications, despite SegDeformer’s computational demands. This improves scalability in the cloud while reducing in-car computational complexity. For the in-car application, we increased the frames per second (fps) by up to a factor of 11.7 (1.4 fps to 16.5 fps) on Cityscapes and by up to a factor of 3.5 (43.3 fps to 154.3 fps) on ADE20K, while being on-par w.r.t. the mean intersection over union (mIoU) of the transformer-based baseline that doesn’t compress by a source codec. For the distributed application, we achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) over a wide range of bitrates on the mIoU metric, while using only 0.14% (0.04%) of cloud DNN parameters used in previous SOTA, reported on ADE20K (Cityscapes).

item.title: SRNet: Semantic Anchoring and Fine-Grained Refinement Network for Semantic Change Detection in Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Zhenghao Jiang
Xiaoqian Zhu
Wanxia Deng
Lei Liu
DongShuo Yin
Biao Wang
Li Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Semantic Change Detection (SCD) identifies changed regions and semantic transitions in bi-temporal remote sensing images, supporting applications such as urban expansion monitoring and land use analysis. Prior studies have achieved notable progress under full parameter training. However, inadequate global semantic consistency and blurred local feature refinement persist. This semantic drift remains a fundamental obstacle to advancing SCD. In this work, we propose the Semantic Anchoring and Fine-Grained Refinement Network (SRNet), a novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) framework. SRNet mitigates semantic drift through a two-stage process of semantic anchoring and fine-grained refinement. Specifically, we introduce a Multi-Scale Fine-Grained Adapter (MA) to adapt pretrained features to the SCD task, achieving semantic anchoring that suppresses cross-temporal feature drift. A Fine-Grained Spatiotemporal Module (FGSM) is then designed to capture subtle local change information across temporal images. We evaluated SRNet on several representative datasets that span various land-cover scenarios, including urban (SECOND, Hi-UCD-mini), rural (HRSCD), and wetland (SJH-SCD) environments. On HRSCD and Hi-UCD-mini, it attains 64.80% and 56.14% Fscd, outperforming the current state-of-the-art models by 3.67% and 6.19%, respectively. Cross-domain transfer from urban to wetland scenes further validates its generalization ability. The code will be available at https://github.com/XiaoJ058/RS-SCD. Published: 2026-07-21T19:04:43+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.766 (consider)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

Relevance: 0.766 (consider)

Semantic Change Detection (SCD) identifies changed regions and semantic transitions in bi-temporal remote sensing images, supporting applications such as urban expansion monitoring and land use analysis. Prior studies have achieved notable progress under full parameter training. However, inadequate global semantic consistency and blurred local feature refinement persist. This semantic drift remains a fundamental obstacle to advancing SCD. In this work, we propose the Semantic Anchoring and Fine-Grained Refinement Network (SRNet), a novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) framework. SRNet mitigates semantic drift through a two-stage process of semantic anchoring and fine-grained refinement. Specifically, we introduce a Multi-Scale Fine-Grained Adapter (MA) to adapt pretrained features to the SCD task, achieving semantic anchoring that suppresses cross-temporal feature drift. A Fine-Grained Spatiotemporal Module (FGSM) is then designed to capture subtle local change information across temporal images. We evaluated SRNet on several representative datasets that span various land-cover scenarios, including urban (SECOND, Hi-UCD-mini), rural (HRSCD), and wetland (SJH-SCD) environments. On HRSCD and Hi-UCD-mini, it attains 64.80% and 56.14% Fscd, outperforming the current state-of-the-art models by 3.67% and 6.19%, respectively. Cross-domain transfer from urban to wetland scenes further validates its generalization ability. The code will be available at https://github.com/XiaoJ058/RS-SCD.

item.title: SceneDreamer360: Text-Driven 3D-Consistent Scene Generation with Panoramic Gaussian Splatting
item.dc:creator: Wenrui Li
Fucheng Cai
Yapeng Mi
Zhe Yang
Wangmeng Zuo
Xingtao Wang
Xiaopeng Fan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
item.description: Text-driven 3D scene generation has seen significant advancements recently. However, most existing methods generate single-view images using generative models and then stitch them together in 3D space. This independent generation for each view often results in spatial inconsistency and implausibility in the 3D scenes. To address this challenge, we proposed a novel text-driven 3D-consistent scene generation model: SceneDreamer360. Our proposed method leverages a text-driven panoramic image generation model as a prior for 3D scene generation and employs 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to ensure consistency across multi-view panoramic images. Specifically, SceneDreamer360 enhances the fine-tuned Panfusion generator with a three-stage panoramic enhancement, enabling the generation of high-resolution, detail-rich panoramic images. During the 3D scene construction, a novel point cloud fusion initialization method is used, producing higher quality and spatially consistent point clouds. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that compared to other methods, SceneDreamer360 with its panoramic image generation and 3DGS can produce higher quality, spatially consistent, and visually appealing 3D scenes from any text prompt. Our codes are available at https://github.com/liwrui/SceneDreamer360. Published: 2026-07-22T19:09:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.764 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wenrui Li; Fucheng Cai; Yapeng Mi; Zhe Yang; Wangmeng Zuo; Xingtao Wang; Xiaopeng Fan

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3716035

Relevance: 0.764 (consider)

Text-driven 3D scene generation has seen significant advancements recently. However, most existing methods generate single-view images using generative models and then stitch them together in 3D space. This independent generation for each view often results in spatial inconsistency and implausibility in the 3D scenes. To address this challenge, we proposed a novel text-driven 3D-consistent scene generation model: SceneDreamer360. Our proposed method leverages a text-driven panoramic image generation model as a prior for 3D scene generation and employs 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to ensure consistency across multi-view panoramic images. Specifically, SceneDreamer360 enhances the fine-tuned Panfusion generator with a three-stage panoramic enhancement, enabling the generation of high-resolution, detail-rich panoramic images. During the 3D scene construction, a novel point cloud fusion initialization method is used, producing higher quality and spatially consistent point clouds. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that compared to other methods, SceneDreamer360 with its panoramic image generation and 3DGS can produce higher quality, spatially consistent, and visually appealing 3D scenes from any text prompt. Our codes are available at https://github.com/liwrui/SceneDreamer360.

item.title: PCSU: A Point Cloud Structure Unification Method for Enhancing Generalization in 3D Object Detection Models
item.dc:creator: Yewei Shi
Baicang Guo
Lisheng Jin
Xiao Yang
Hongyu Zhang
Qingsong Wei
Guofeng Luo
Menglin Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Cross-domain generalization remains a critical challenge in LiDAR-based 3D object detection, primarily due to significant variations in point cloud distributions caused by different sensor configurations, installation angles, and environmental conditions. While prior research has focused extensively on network-level adaptations, the role of point cloud preprocessing has received limited attention. In this paper, we present a systematic study on how structural differences in raw point clouds affect the generalization performance of 3D detection models, and we propose a Point Cloud Structure Unification (PCSU) framework to mitigate these disparities. PCSU comprises three modular components: height normalization to align vertical distributions, reflectivity suppression to reduce overfitting to sensor-specific intensity patterns, and resolution standardization via voxel center alignment. Through extensive experiments across multiple datasets and detection models, we demonstrate that these structure-aware preprocessing strategies generally enhance cross-domain generalization without modifying model architectures. The improvements are most prominent for larger objects and near-to-moderate ranges. Our findings highlight the often-overlooked importance of data-level harmonization and provide actionable insights for designing more generalizable 3D detection pipelines. Published: 2026-07-21T19:10:51+00:00 Venue: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.763 (consider)
