Authors: Yewei Shi; Baicang Guo; Lisheng Jin; Xiao Yang; Hongyu Zhang; Qingsong Wei; Guofeng Luo; Menglin Li

Journal: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems

DOI: 10.1109/ojits.2026.3715413

Relevance: 0.763 (consider)

Cross-domain generalization remains a critical challenge in LiDAR-based 3D object detection, primarily due to significant variations in point cloud distributions caused by different sensor configurations, installation angles, and environmental conditions. While prior research has focused extensively on network-level adaptations, the role of point cloud preprocessing has received limited attention. In this paper, we present a systematic study on how structural differences in raw point clouds affect the generalization performance of 3D detection models, and we propose a Point Cloud Structure Unification (PCSU) framework to mitigate these disparities. PCSU comprises three modular components: height normalization to align vertical distributions, reflectivity suppression to reduce overfitting to sensor-specific intensity patterns, and resolution standardization via voxel center alignment. Through extensive experiments across multiple datasets and detection models, we demonstrate that these structure-aware preprocessing strategies generally enhance cross-domain generalization without modifying model architectures. The improvements are most prominent for larger objects and near-to-moderate ranges. Our findings highlight the often-overlooked importance of data-level harmonization and provide actionable insights for designing more generalizable 3D detection pipelines.

item.title: Jamming Pattern Recognition for Radar Based on Phase Space Reconstruction and Dynamic Characteristics
item.dc:creator: Tao Xu
Fucheng Guo
Weidong Hu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: We propose a mechanism-guided radar jamming recognition framework based on phase space reconstruction (PSR). We formulate a discrete-time digital radio frequency memory (DRFM) state-machine model that represents memory write/read addressing, sampling/forwarding mode switching, numerically controlled oscillator (NCO) phase accumulation, digital gain control (DGC), and optional noise-branch activation. The model describes jamming patterns as control-word strategies of a common physical generator rather than as independent empirical equations. The emitted waveform is embedded in a reconstructed phase space, from which geometric descriptors and Gaussian mixture model (GMM) phase-point distribution features are extracted and classified by a random forest (RF). Simulation and measured-data experiments show that the proposed features improve robustness at low jamming-to-noise ratio (JNR) compared with conventional time-, frequency-, and time-frequency-domain features, while providing competitive performance relative to deep learning methods and retaining physical interpretability. Published: 2026-07-22T19:04:36+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.759 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tao Xu; Fucheng Guo; Weidong Hu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3716239

Relevance: 0.759 (consider)

We propose a mechanism-guided radar jamming recognition framework based on phase space reconstruction (PSR). We formulate a discrete-time digital radio frequency memory (DRFM) state-machine model that represents memory write/read addressing, sampling/forwarding mode switching, numerically controlled oscillator (NCO) phase accumulation, digital gain control (DGC), and optional noise-branch activation. The model describes jamming patterns as control-word strategies of a common physical generator rather than as independent empirical equations. The emitted waveform is embedded in a reconstructed phase space, from which geometric descriptors and Gaussian mixture model (GMM) phase-point distribution features are extracted and classified by a random forest (RF). Simulation and measured-data experiments show that the proposed features improve robustness at low jamming-to-noise ratio (JNR) compared with conventional time-, frequency-, and time-frequency-domain features, while providing competitive performance relative to deep learning methods and retaining physical interpretability.

item.title: Physics-Driven Resolution Refinement for Electrical Resistivity Tomography Using Multi-Array Learning and Vision Mamba
item.dc:creator: Yinpeng Li
Xianghao Liu
Yanqi Wu
Zhijun Huo
Zhuo Jia
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) inversion is inherently nonlinear and ill-posed, and conventional algorithms often suffer from limited resolution due to their dependence on initial models and regularization. Recent deep learning (DL)-based approaches have shown potential for resolution enhancement, but purely data-driven methods lack physical interpretability, while physics-constrained networks typically provide limited improvement and incur high computational cost. To address these challenges, we propose a physics-driven resolution refinement framework for ERT. The core idea is to learn a differentiable surrogate forward model from multi-array observations and use it as a physical guide to refine conventional inversion results. Specifically, a multi-array forward network is trained to jointly predict responses from Wenner-α, dipole–dipole, and Schlumberger arrays, capturing their complementary sensitivities through shared representation learning. Based on this learned forward mapping, the conventional inversion model is iteratively refined by enforcing cross-array response consistency, while an adaptively weighted supervision term is incorporated to stabilize the update. A Vision Mamba backbone is adopted to enhance feature representation and improve the recovery of fine-scale structures. Experiments on both synthetic and field data demonstrate that the proposed method improves anomaly delineation and structural detail recovery under the tested synthetic and field settings. Meanwhile, it enhances cross-array physical consistency and avoids the computational overhead associated with repeated numerical forward modeling. These results highlight the effectiveness of integrating learned forward physics with data-driven refinement for high-resolution ERT imaging. Published: 2026-07-21T19:04:46+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.759 (consider)

Authors: Yinpeng Li; Xianghao Liu; Yanqi Wu; Zhijun Huo; Zhuo Jia

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3715567

Relevance: 0.759 (consider)

Electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) inversion is inherently nonlinear and ill-posed, and conventional algorithms often suffer from limited resolution due to their dependence on initial models and regularization. Recent deep learning (DL)-based approaches have shown potential for resolution enhancement, but purely data-driven methods lack physical interpretability, while physics-constrained networks typically provide limited improvement and incur high computational cost. To address these challenges, we propose a physics-driven resolution refinement framework for ERT. The core idea is to learn a differentiable surrogate forward model from multi-array observations and use it as a physical guide to refine conventional inversion results. Specifically, a multi-array forward network is trained to jointly predict responses from Wenner-α, dipole–dipole, and Schlumberger arrays, capturing their complementary sensitivities through shared representation learning. Based on this learned forward mapping, the conventional inversion model is iteratively refined by enforcing cross-array response consistency, while an adaptively weighted supervision term is incorporated to stabilize the update. A Vision Mamba backbone is adopted to enhance feature representation and improve the recovery of fine-scale structures. Experiments on both synthetic and field data demonstrate that the proposed method improves anomaly delineation and structural detail recovery under the tested synthetic and field settings. Meanwhile, it enhances cross-array physical consistency and avoids the computational overhead associated with repeated numerical forward modeling. These results highlight the effectiveness of integrating learned forward physics with data-driven refinement for high-resolution ERT imaging.

item.title: A deep transfer learning method for generating high spatiotemporal resolution leaf area index from Landsat surface reflectance
item.dc:creator: Guodong Zhang
Gaofei Yin
Yizhi Wang
Yi Zhang
Jiangchuan Hu
Gang Sun
Yimin Ni
Rui Chen
Wei Zhao
Meilian Wang
Yufang Zhang
Anxin Ding
Xinyan Liu
Aleixandre Verger
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Leaf area index (LAI) is an important biophysical parameter characterizing vegetation structure and function in ecosystem modeling and climate studies. While medium-resolution LAI products have been widely used for global monitoring, they cannot adequately capture fine-scale heterogeneity. High-resolution satellite data provide new opportunities for LAI estimation, but two major challenges remain: (1) significant discrepancies between satellite-derived and ground-measured LAI due to reliance on satellite products or simulation data for model training, and (2) time series discontinuity caused by cloud contamination. To address these challenges, this study proposes a deep transfer learning framework to generate seamless high spatiotemporal resolution LAI. The framework consists of three steps: First, a Deep Belief Network (DBN) model is pre-trained using coarse-resolution satellite products to establish universal spectral-LAI relationships. Second, the model is fine-tuned with global ground measurements from ImagineS and GBOV networks, effectively bridging the gap between satellite retrievals and field observations. Third, a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM)-based reconstruction model integrating spatiotemporal constraints and environmental variables fills cloud-induced gaps to achieve continuous LAI time series. Comprehensive validation using field measurements across seven land cover types from 2013 to 2021 demonstrates significant accuracy improvements (overall Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) = 1.06–1.11), with particularly notable enhancements in high-LAI forests (RMSE reduced from 1.47–1.67 to 0.81–0.95). Compared with existing spatiotemporal reconstruction methods, our approach achieves superior spatial detail preservation and temporal continuity. As a demonstration, we generated a seamless 30-m, 8-day LAI product for China spanning 2013–2021. This study highlights the value of deep transfer learning for cross-scale vegetation parameter retrieval by combi... Published: 2026-07-22T19:07:55+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.758 (consider)

Leaf area index (LAI) is an important biophysical parameter characterizing vegetation structure and function in ecosystem modeling and climate studies. While medium-resolution LAI products have been widely used for global monitoring, they cannot adequately capture fine-scale heterogeneity. High-resolution satellite data provide new opportunities for LAI estimation, but two major challenges remain: (1) significant discrepancies between satellite-derived and ground-measured LAI due to reliance on satellite products or simulation data for model training, and (2) time series discontinuity caused by cloud contamination. To address these challenges, this study proposes a deep transfer learning framework to generate seamless high spatiotemporal resolution LAI. The framework consists of three steps: First, a Deep Belief Network (DBN) model is pre-trained using coarse-resolution satellite products to establish universal spectral-LAI relationships. Second, the model is fine-tuned with global ground measurements from ImagineS and GBOV networks, effectively bridging the gap between satellite retrievals and field observations. Third, a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM)-based reconstruction model integrating spatiotemporal constraints and environmental variables fills cloud-induced gaps to achieve continuous LAI time series. Comprehensive validation using field measurements across seven land cover types from 2013 to 2021 demonstrates significant accuracy improvements (overall Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) = 1.06–1.11), with particularly notable enhancements in high-LAI forests (RMSE reduced from 1.47–1.67 to 0.81–0.95). Compared with existing spatiotemporal reconstruction methods, our approach achieves superior spatial detail preservation and temporal continuity. As a demonstration, we generated a seamless 30-m, 8-day LAI product for China spanning 2013–2021. This study highlights the value of deep transfer learning for cross-scale vegetation parameter retrieval by combi...

item.title: Two-Stage Homotopic Learning for Data-Driven Cluster Consensus in Multiagent Systems
item.dc:creator: Qing Yang
Jing Wang
Jinde Cao
Leszek Rutkowski
Hao Shen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
item.description: In this article, a novel homotopic reinforcement learning (RL) framework is proposed to address the distributed cluster consensus problem in continuous-time multiagent systems (MASs). For the first time, the investigated issue is formulated as a zero-sum differential game using a proposed minmax game policy, in which a local regulation error is introduced to characterize the deviation of each agent from its assigned leader. Based on this formulation, a set of group game algebraic Riccati equations is derived to obtain the optimal control law. To overcome reliance on known system models, these equations are solved using a data-driven homotopic policy-iteration scheme that leverages online state and input information. In contrast to conventional learning schemes, the proposed approach embeds a homotopic process that relaxes the requirement for an admissible initial policy. Rigorous stability and convergence analyses are provided, and the effectiveness of the proposed method is further demonstrated through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations. Published: 2026-07-21T19:03:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics Score: 0.754 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qing Yang; Jing Wang; Jinde Cao; Leszek Rutkowski; Hao Shen

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics

DOI: 10.1109/tcyb.2026.3710815

Relevance: 0.754 (consider)

In this article, a novel homotopic reinforcement learning (RL) framework is proposed to address the distributed cluster consensus problem in continuous-time multiagent systems (MASs). For the first time, the investigated issue is formulated as a zero-sum differential game using a proposed minmax game policy, in which a local regulation error is introduced to characterize the deviation of each agent from its assigned leader. Based on this formulation, a set of group game algebraic Riccati equations is derived to obtain the optimal control law. To overcome reliance on known system models, these equations are solved using a data-driven homotopic policy-iteration scheme that leverages online state and input information. In contrast to conventional learning schemes, the proposed approach embeds a homotopic process that relaxes the requirement for an admissible initial policy. Rigorous stability and convergence analyses are provided, and the effectiveness of the proposed method is further demonstrated through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations.

item.title: Seeing Beyond the Present: A Future-Aware Privileged Knowledge Distillation Framework for Vehicle Trajectory Prediction
item.dc:creator: Yuxi Xue
Chao Wang
Xiaobo Chen
Jianjun Qian
Jian Yang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
item.description: Vehicle trajectory prediction serves as a core component in enhancing the safety of autonomous driving systems. Most existing methods focus on extracting historical context from trajectory data, while overlooking the importance of future context. To address this limitation, we propose a generic future-aware privileged knowledge distillation (FAPKD) framework to enhance existing trajectory prediction models that lack the capability of capturing potential future interactions. FAPKD includes a teacher, a student, and a knowledge distillation module. The teacher accesses true future states of neighboring vehicles as privileged information to capture real future context, while the student learns to infer it from past observations. The distillation module, with a multi-stage design and a distillation timestep selector (DTS), ensures that the student effectively learns to predict future context. Furthermore, to mitigate the prediction drift that often arises when applying Mean Squared Error (MSE)-based distillation to trajectory data, we introduce a wavelet-based trajectory distillation method. This method decomposes trajectory sequences into wavelet coefficients and subsequently represents them as probability distributions. By operating at the distribution level, the student is guided to internalize the teacher's latent knowledge while not deviating from the true future trajectory. Experimental results on the NGSIM, HighD and Argoverse 1 datasets demonstrate that our framework is model-agnostic and can be seamlessly integrated into existing encoder-decoder-based trajectory prediction models, leading to notable performance improvements. Published: 2026-07-22T19:07:14+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.753 (consider)
item.content:encoded:

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3716040

Relevance: 0.753 (consider)

Vehicle trajectory prediction serves as a core component in enhancing the safety of autonomous driving systems. Most existing methods focus on extracting historical context from trajectory data, while overlooking the importance of future context. To address this limitation, we propose a generic future-aware privileged knowledge distillation (FAPKD) framework to enhance existing trajectory prediction models that lack the capability of capturing potential future interactions. FAPKD includes a teacher, a student, and a knowledge distillation module. The teacher accesses true future states of neighboring vehicles as privileged information to capture real future context, while the student learns to infer it from past observations. The distillation module, with a multi-stage design and a distillation timestep selector (DTS), ensures that the student effectively learns to predict future context. Furthermore, to mitigate the prediction drift that often arises when applying Mean Squared Error (MSE)-based distillation to trajectory data, we introduce a wavelet-based trajectory distillation method. This method decomposes trajectory sequences into wavelet coefficients and subsequently represents them as probability distributions. By operating at the distribution level, the student is guided to internalize the teacher's latent knowledge while not deviating from the true future trajectory. Experimental results on the NGSIM, HighD and Argoverse 1 datasets demonstrate that our framework is model-agnostic and can be seamlessly integrated into existing encoder-decoder-based trajectory prediction models, leading to notable performance improvements.

item.title: Power Battery Detection
item.dc:creator: Xiaoqi Zhao
Peiqian Cao
Chenyang Yu
Zonglei Feng
Lihe Zhang
Hanqi Liu
Jiaming Zuo
Youwei Pang
Jinsong Ouyang
Weisi Lin
Georges El Fakhri
Huchuan Lu
Xiaofeng Liu
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Computer Vision
item.description: Power batteries are essential components in electric vehicles, where internal structural defects can pose serious safety risks. We conduct a comprehensive study on a new task, power battery detection (PBD), which aims to localize the dense endpoints of cathode and anode plates from industrial X-ray images for quality inspection. Manual inspection is inefficient and error-prone, while traditional vision algorithms struggle with densely packed plates, low contrast, scale variation, and imaging artifacts. To address this issue and drive more attention into this meaningful task, we present PBD5K, the first large-scale benchmark for this task, consisting of 5,000 X-ray images from nine battery types with fine-grained annotations and eight types of real-world visual interference. To support scalable and consistent labeling, we develop an intelligent annotation pipeline that combines image filtering, model-assisted pre-labeling, cross-verification, and layered quality evaluation. We formulate PBD as a point-level segmentation problem and propose MDCNeXt, a model designed to extract and integrate multi-dimensional structure clues including point, line, and count information from the plate itself. To improve discrimination between plates and suppress visual interference, MDCNeXt incorporates two state space modules. The first is a prompt-filtered module that learns contrastive relationships guided by task-specific prompts. The second is a density-aware reordering module that refines segmentation in regions with high plate density. In addition, we propose a distance-adaptive mask generation strategy to provide robust supervision under varying spatial distributions of anode and cathode positions. Without any bells and whistles, our segmentation-based MDCNeXt consistently outperforms various other corner detection, crowd counting and general/tiny object detection-based solutions, making it a strong baseline that can help facilitate future research in PBD. Finally, we provide a detailed discussion of future directions and challenges for research in power battery detection. Published: 2026-07-22T07:20:50+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.753 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaoqi Zhao; Peiqian Cao; Chenyang Yu; Zonglei Feng; Lihe Zhang; Hanqi Liu; Jiaming Zuo; Youwei Pang; Jinsong Ouyang; Weisi Lin; Georges El Fakhri; Huchuan Lu; Xiaofeng Liu

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02919-8

Relevance: 0.753 (consider)

Power batteries are essential components in electric vehicles, where internal structural defects can pose serious safety risks. We conduct a comprehensive study on a new task, power battery detection (PBD), which aims to localize the dense endpoints of cathode and anode plates from industrial X-ray images for quality inspection. Manual inspection is inefficient and error-prone, while traditional vision algorithms struggle with densely packed plates, low contrast, scale variation, and imaging artifacts. To address this issue and drive more attention into this meaningful task, we present PBD5K, the first large-scale benchmark for this task, consisting of 5,000 X-ray images from nine battery types with fine-grained annotations and eight types of real-world visual interference. To support scalable and consistent labeling, we develop an intelligent annotation pipeline that combines image filtering, model-assisted pre-labeling, cross-verification, and layered quality evaluation. We formulate PBD as a point-level segmentation problem and propose MDCNeXt, a model designed to extract and integrate multi-dimensional structure clues including point, line, and count information from the plate itself. To improve discrimination between plates and suppress visual interference, MDCNeXt incorporates two state space modules. The first is a prompt-filtered module that learns contrastive relationships guided by task-specific prompts. The second is a density-aware reordering module that refines segmentation in regions with high plate density. In addition, we propose a distance-adaptive mask generation strategy to provide robust supervision under varying spatial distributions of anode and cathode positions. Without any bells and whistles, our segmentation-based MDCNeXt consistently outperforms various other corner detection, crowd counting and general/tiny object detection-based solutions, making it a strong baseline that can help facilitate future research in PBD. Finally, we provide a detailed discussion of future directions and challenges for research in power battery detection.

item.title: Human-Inspired Graphformer-Tutor: Accelerating and Safeguarding Multi-Agent Driving Learning
item.dc:creator: Fan Guo
Chuyu Ma
Dezong Zhao
Jiayang Wang
Zhengqing Yan
Jinbo Hao
Yun Chen
Lianhui Zhang
Kang Song
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Realistic modeling of interactive behaviors among multiple vehicles is essential for autonomous driving simulation. While Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) provides a promising framework for this task, the pure trial-and-error exploration paradigm often leads to inefficient and biased learning processes, limiting its ability to reproduce realistic driving behaviors. In light of this, inspired by the Tutor–Students learning paradigm and leveraging human expertise, a novel MARL algorithm termed Tutor–Multiple-Students Proximal Policy Optimization (TMSPPO) is proposed. A Graphformer-based tutor agent is developed to capture spatio-temporal driving features from human data and provide anthropomorphic assistance. TMSPPO embeds this tutor agent into the MARL loop through two complementary mechanisms: policy guidance and safety protection. Specifically, the guidance mechanism prevents student agents from cold-starting by integrating seamlessly with exploration, while the safety protection mechanism filters extreme samples yet retains informative near-misses to capture corrective data in near-danger scenarios. To further achieve a principled trade-off between autonomous exploration and tutor dependence, the intervention frequency and intensity are formulated as constrained objectives co-optimized with the learning goal. Consequently, TMSPPO substantially enhances learning efficiency and interactive driving capability, enabling student agents to display diverse and human-like behaviors in complex scenarios. Published: 2026-07-22T19:08:03+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.752 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fan Guo; Chuyu Ma; Dezong Zhao; Jiayang Wang; Zhengqing Yan; Jinbo Hao; Yun Chen; Lianhui Zhang; Kang Song

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tits.2026.3709675

Relevance: 0.752 (consider)

Realistic modeling of interactive behaviors among multiple vehicles is essential for autonomous driving simulation. While Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) provides a promising framework for this task, the pure trial-and-error exploration paradigm often leads to inefficient and biased learning processes, limiting its ability to reproduce realistic driving behaviors. In light of this, inspired by the Tutor–Students learning paradigm and leveraging human expertise, a novel MARL algorithm termed Tutor–Multiple-Students Proximal Policy Optimization (TMSPPO) is proposed. A Graphformer-based tutor agent is developed to capture spatio-temporal driving features from human data and provide anthropomorphic assistance. TMSPPO embeds this tutor agent into the MARL loop through two complementary mechanisms: policy guidance and safety protection. Specifically, the guidance mechanism prevents student agents from cold-starting by integrating seamlessly with exploration, while the safety protection mechanism filters extreme samples yet retains informative near-misses to capture corrective data in near-danger scenarios. To further achieve a principled trade-off between autonomous exploration and tutor dependence, the intervention frequency and intensity are formulated as constrained objectives co-optimized with the learning goal. Consequently, TMSPPO substantially enhances learning efficiency and interactive driving capability, enabling student agents to display diverse and human-like behaviors in complex scenarios.
